sharks.name: Whale Shark

earlyReader: This is a whale shark.
It is the biggest fish in the world.
Its spots help us know each shark.
story: The spotted whale shark is the biggest fish in the world.
It swims steadily through warm seas, searching for tiny food.

earlyReader: Whale sharks like warm water.
They swim near sunny coasts.
Some cross wide oceans.
story: Whale sharks travel through warm tropical seas and gather where food is plentiful.
Some make long trips across the open ocean.

earlyReader: Tiny plankton drifts in the sea.
The shark opens its wide mouth.
Water streams past its gills.
story: A whale shark filters tiny plankton from great mouthfuls of water.
The water streams across special pads near its gills.

earlyReader: This shark can grow very long.
It can be longer than a bus.
It is still a gentle swimmer.
story: A full-grown whale shark can stretch longer than a school bus.
Its enormous size makes its slow, steady swimming look peaceful.

earlyReader: The shark can smell food.
It spots busy patches of water.
Then it steers toward plankton.
story: Whale sharks use smell and other senses to locate rich patches of plankton.
They steer toward places where tiny animals gather.

earlyReader: Its mouth works like a filter.
Food stays in.
Water splashes back to the sea.
story: The whale shark's special power is filter feeding.
It keeps tiny food while sending clean water back through its gills.

traits.title: Giant Filter Mouth
traits.description: It opens wide and filters tiny plankton from the water.

traits.title: Constellation Spots
traits.description: Its white spots make a pattern as special as a fingerprint.

mission.title: Plankton Picnic

earlyReader: Steer toward the bright plankton.
Tap each sparkling cloud.
Help the shark eat its tiny food.
story: Guide the whale shark through sparkling clouds of plankton.
Collect each cloud to finish its gentle ocean meal.

mission.completion: You found the plankton! The whale shark is a filter feeder.

earlyReader: What does the whale shark eat?
story: Which tiny food does the whale shark filter from the water?

questions.success: You found it! Whale sharks eat plankton.
questions.retry: Good guess. Look for the tiny sparkling animals.

earlyReader: Where does this shark swim?
story: Which water does a whale shark usually choose?

questions.success: Yes! Whale sharks swim in warm oceans.
questions.retry: Let's look again. Choose the warm ocean.

earlyReader: Which shark is the biggest fish?
story: Which animal is the biggest fish in the world?

questions.success: That's right! The whale shark is the biggest fish.
questions.retry: Good thinking. Look for the very big shark.

sharks.bookFact: The biggest fish eats some of the ocean's tiniest animals.

sharks.name: Great Hammerhead

earlyReader: This is a hammerhead shark.
Its head is wide.
It can spot food under sand.
story: The great hammerhead swings its wide head from side to side.
Special sensors help it spot prey hidden under the sandy seafloor.

earlyReader: Hammerheads like warm seas.
They visit reefs and sandy bays.
They swim near coasts and islands.
story: Great hammerheads roam warm seas near reefs, islands, and sandy coasts.
They also cross stretches of open ocean.

earlyReader: Hammerheads eat fish and crabs.
They also hunt stingrays.
A ray may hide under sand.
story: Great hammerheads eat fish, crabs, squid, and stingrays.
They can track a stingray even when it stays hidden beneath sand.

earlyReader: This hammerhead grows very big.
Its wide head can steer through water.
Its tall fin stands above its back.
story: The great hammerhead is the largest hammerhead species.
Its broad head and tall dorsal fin make an easy-to-spot shape.

earlyReader: Animals make tiny electric signals.
Special pores can sense them.
The shark sweeps its head to search.
story: Electroreception lets a hammerhead sense faint electric fields made by living animals.
Sweeping its head helps it search a wide strip of seafloor.

earlyReader: Its wide head holds many sensors.
It steers from side to side.
Hidden prey cannot stay hidden.
story: The hammer-shaped head spreads the shark's electric sensors across a broad surface.
That special shape helps reveal prey beneath the sand.

traits.title: Hammer Head Sensors
traits.description: Tiny sensors in its wide head help it find hidden animals.

traits.title: Wide-Eye View
traits.description: Eyes at the ends of its head help it see a broad view.

mission.title: Electric Search

earlyReader: Sweep across the sandy floor.
Tap each electric sparkle.
Find the hidden stingray clues.
story: Use the hammerhead's electroreception to scan the sandy seafloor.
Follow each electric clue toward hidden prey.

mission.completion: You sensed it! Electroreception finds animals hidden under sand.

earlyReader: What is wide on this shark?
story: Which part gives the hammerhead its name?

questions.success: Yes! The hammerhead has a wide head.
questions.retry: Good guess. Look for the wide shape.

earlyReader: What can hide under sand?
story: Which prey can a hammerhead sense beneath sand?

questions.success: You found it! A stingray can hide under sand.
questions.retry: Let's scan again for the flat ocean animal.

earlyReader: Which sense finds electric signals?
story: What is the shark's electric sense called?

questions.success: Exactly! Electroreception detects electric signals.
questions.retry: Good thinking. Choose the electric sparkle.

sharks.bookFact: Its wide head sweeps over sand like a living metal detector.

sharks.name: Nurse Shark

earlyReader: This is a nurse shark.
It rests on the ocean floor.
It stays still and pumps water over its gills.
story: The nurse shark rests quietly on the ocean floor during the day.
It can stay still because it pumps water across its gills.

earlyReader: Nurse sharks live near reefs.
They rest in caves or on sand.
Some return to the same spot.
story: Nurse sharks live around warm reefs, sandy flats, and mangroves.
They often return to a favorite resting spot after hunting.

earlyReader: A nurse shark hunts at night.
It eats crabs, squid, and fish.
It can slurp food from a crack.
story: At night, nurse sharks search for crabs, squid, shellfish, and small fish.
Powerful suction pulls food from narrow reef crevices.

earlyReader: A nurse shark is long and strong.
Its head is broad and flat.
Its tail is very long.
story: Nurse sharks have broad heads, sturdy bodies, and long tails.
Adults can grow longer than a tall person lying down.

earlyReader: Two barbels hang by its mouth.
They feel for food below.
The shark spots prey in dark cracks.
story: Whisker-like barbels near the mouth help a nurse shark find food.
Smell and touch guide it while it searches dark reef cracks.

earlyReader: The shark makes strong suction.
Food slips out of a crack.
Then small teeth crush shells.
story: A nurse shark can create sudden, powerful suction at its mouth.
Rows of small teeth help it crush hard-shelled prey.

traits.title: Suction Supper
traits.description: Its round mouth can pull a crab or shellfish from a reef crevice.

traits.title: Reef Rest
traits.description: A nurse shark often rests calmly on the sea floor.

mission.title: Night Reef Snack

earlyReader: Use the barbels to search.
Spot the crabs in reef cracks.
Tap to slurp each one.
story: Search the night reef with the nurse shark's sensitive barbels.
Find each crab and use suction to pull it from hiding.

mission.completion: Slurp! The nurse shark used suction to find its food.

earlyReader: Where does the nurse shark rest?
story: Where does a nurse shark often rest during the day?

questions.success: You found it! The ocean floor is its resting place.
questions.retry: Good guess. Look low, beneath the water.

earlyReader: What hangs by its mouth?
story: Which feelers help the nurse shark search?

questions.success: Right! Barbels help it find food.
questions.retry: Look for the two little feelers.

earlyReader: When does it hunt?
story: When is a nurse shark usually most active?

questions.success: Yes! Nurse sharks often hunt at night.
questions.retry: Let's look again for the moon.

sharks.bookFact: It can breathe while resting quietly on the seafloor.

sharks.name: Great White Shark

earlyReader: This is a great white shark.
It is a strong swimmer.
It can spot shapes above the water.
story: The great white is a strong predator of cool coastal seas.
It watches silhouettes above and can burst forward with surprising speed.

earlyReader: Great whites like cool coastal water.
They also cross open oceans.
Some migrate very far.
story: Great white sharks patrol cool and temperate coasts around the world.
They can migrate thousands of miles across open ocean.

earlyReader: Young sharks eat fish and rays.
Large sharks may hunt seals.
A predator hunts other animals.
story: Young great whites mostly eat fish, squid, and rays.
Larger sharks can hunt marine mammals such as seals.

earlyReader: A great white grows very big.
Females grow bigger than males.
Its body is shaped for speed.
story: Great whites are among the largest hunting sharks alive today.
Their streamlined bodies and strong tails power quick bursts of speed.

earlyReader: This shark can see well.
It can smell and hear prey.
Electric sensors help up close.
story: Vision, smell, hearing, and electroreception all help a great white investigate its surroundings.
Each sense works across a different distance.

earlyReader: The shark starts deep below.
It speeds toward a shape above.
Sometimes it splashes out of the sea.
story: A great white can accelerate upward from deeper water toward prey at the surface.
The powerful rush may carry the shark clear of the water.

traits.title: Triangle Teeth
traits.description: Rows of triangle-shaped teeth help it hold slippery prey.

traits.title: Surface Silhouette
traits.description: From below, it can spot dark shapes against bright water.

mission.title: Silhouette Search

earlyReader: Start deep below.
Spot each shadow above.
Tap the clues to build speed.
story: Watch the bright surface for prey-shaped silhouettes.
Follow each clue upward and tag the final silhouette without showing a bite.

mission.completion: Great spotting! The shark used light and shadow to search above.

earlyReader: Which water does it often like?
story: Where do many great white sharks patrol?

questions.success: Yes! Great whites often patrol cool coasts.
questions.retry: Good guess. Choose the cool ocean coast.

earlyReader: What does a young shark eat?
story: Which food is common for a young great white?

questions.success: That's it! Young great whites eat fish.
questions.retry: Look for an animal that swims.

earlyReader: What helps it swim fast?
story: Which body part powers a great white's speed?

questions.success: Right! Its strong tail pushes it forward.
questions.retry: Try the body part that swings behind the shark.

sharks.bookFact: It can travel across entire oceans and return to rich feeding grounds.

sharks.name: Spotted Wobbegong

earlyReader: This is a spotted wobbegong.
It stays still on the seafloor.
Its spots help it disappear.
story: The spotted wobbegong rests flat against the rocky seafloor.
Its carpet-like pattern is camouflage that helps it almost disappear.

earlyReader: Wobbegongs live near rocky reefs.
They rest in caves and under ledges.
They stay close to the seafloor.
story: Spotted wobbegongs live on rocky and coral reefs around southern Australia.
They rest beneath ledges and inside caves during the day.

earlyReader: A wobbegong waits for food.
Fish or octopus may swim close.
Then the shark strikes fast.
story: A hidden wobbegong waits for fish, octopus, or crustaceans to approach.
Its mouth opens quickly and pulls nearby prey inward.

earlyReader: This shark is wide and flat.
Its tail is shorter than many sharks.
Skin flaps fringe its head.
story: The spotted wobbegong has a broad, flattened body and a patterned back.
Branched skin flaps around its head soften its outline.

earlyReader: It can smell prey nearby.
Small barbels help it search.
It spots movement from its hiding place.
story: Smell, vision, and small barbels help the wobbegong notice prey.
It stays alert even while resting motionless.

earlyReader: Camouflage helps it blend in.
Spots match rocks and sand.
The shark stays hidden and still.
story: Camouflage breaks up the wobbegong's outline against rocks and sand.
Stillness makes the disguise even more effective.

traits.title: Leafy Camouflage
traits.description: Skin flaps and spots help it look like part of the reef.

traits.title: Bottom Hider
traits.description: It rests flat on the sea floor and waits quietly.

mission.title: Seafloor Hide-and-Seek

earlyReader: Study the spotted seafloor.
Find each shape that blends in.
Tap the hidden clues.
story: Look closely at the patterned seafloor for shapes that do not quite belong.
Find every clue to reveal the camouflaged wobbegong.

mission.completion: You found it! Camouflage helps the wobbegong blend in.

earlyReader: What helps it blend in?
story: What makes the wobbegong hard to see?

questions.success: Yes! Camouflage helps it blend in.
questions.retry: Look for the pattern that hides an animal.

earlyReader: Where does it rest?
story: Which place matches a wobbegong's flat body?

questions.success: Right! It rests on the seafloor.
questions.retry: Good guess. Choose the bottom of the sea.

earlyReader: How does it catch food?
story: What does a hidden wobbegong do when prey comes close?

questions.success: Exactly! It waits, then strikes quickly.
questions.retry: Look for the fast action from a hiding place.

sharks.bookFact: Its carpet-like spots and skin flaps hide its shape among rocks.

sharks.name: Thresher Shark

earlyReader: This is a thresher shark.
Its tail is very long.
It can strike fish with a splash.
story: The thresher shark carries an upper tail lobe nearly as long as its body.
A swift tail strike can stun schooling fish.

earlyReader: Threshers swim in open water.
They visit deep and coastal seas.
Some travel far for food.
story: Thresher sharks range through temperate and tropical oceans.
They move between coastal feeding grounds and deeper open water.

earlyReader: Threshers eat schooling fish.
They also eat squid.
A tight school makes a good target.
story: Sardines, anchovies, mackerel, and squid are common thresher prey.
The shark first helps crowd schooling fish together.

earlyReader: Half its length can be tail.
The top tail lobe is extra long.
Its body stays sleek for speed.
story: A thresher's upper tail lobe may be as long as the rest of the shark.
That striking shape makes threshers easy to recognize.

earlyReader: Large eyes spot fish in dim water.
The shark senses a moving school.
Then it steers into place.
story: Thresher sharks use vision and vibration senses to track moving schools.
Their eyes help when light grows dim in deeper water.

earlyReader: The tail sweeps forward.
It strikes with a strong snap.
Stunned fish are easier to catch.
story: A thresher accelerates its tail over its head like a whip.
The strike can stun several closely packed fish at once.

traits.title: Super-Long Tail
traits.description: Its upper tail fin can be as long as its body.

traits.title: Tail Slap
traits.description: A quick tail slap can bunch small fish together.

mission.title: Tail-Strike Timing

earlyReader: Wait for fish to school together.
Tap each fish clue.
Build one strong tail strike.
story: Gather the fish into a tight school and watch their movement.
Time the thresher's long tail strike when the school is together.

mission.completion: Splash! The thresher used its long tail to stun schooling fish.

earlyReader: Which part is extra long?
story: Which body part makes a thresher easy to recognize?

questions.success: Yes! The thresher has an extra-long tail.
questions.retry: Look behind the shark for the long part.

earlyReader: What does the tail do?
story: How does the thresher use its tail to hunt?

questions.success: Right! Its tail strike can stun fish.
questions.retry: Choose the splashy, powerful action.

earlyReader: Which food swims in a school?
story: Which prey gathers in a school?

questions.success: Exactly! Small fish swim in schools.
questions.retry: Look for the group of swimming animals.

sharks.bookFact: Its whip-like tail can stun several schooling fish at once.

sharks.name: Tiger Shark

earlyReader: This is a tiger shark.
Young sharks have dark stripes.
It uses strong senses to search murky water.
story: Young tiger sharks wear dark stripes that fade as they grow.
Strong smell and hearing help this traveler search murky coastal water.

earlyReader: Tiger sharks like warm seas.
They visit reefs, bays, and open water.
Some migrate between islands.
story: Tiger sharks use many warm-water habitats, from bays and reefs to open ocean.
Seasonal travelers may migrate great distances between islands.

earlyReader: Tiger sharks eat many ocean animals.
Fish, rays, and turtles are prey.
They also scavenge food they find.
story: Tiger sharks eat fish, rays, turtles, seabirds, squid, and crustaceans.
They are also scavengers that investigate many possible meals.

earlyReader: A tiger shark can grow large.
Its head is wide and blunt.
Its strong body narrows toward the tail.
story: Tiger sharks are among the ocean's largest hunting sharks.
A broad head and powerful body support short bursts of speed.

earlyReader: Smell helps find food far away.
Hearing spots splashes and movement.
Other senses guide the final search.
story: Tiger sharks use long-range smell and hearing to locate possible food.
Vision, vibration sensing, and electroreception help at closer range.

earlyReader: A scent can spread through murky water.
The shark follows the trail.
It spots food with many senses.
story: The tiger shark combines several senses while investigating cloudy water.
A distant scent or low sound can start the search.

traits.title: Tiger Stripes
traits.description: Dark stripes along its sides inspired its tiger name.

traits.title: Scent Tracker
traits.description: It can follow tiny scent clues through the water.

mission.title: Murky-Water Trail

earlyReader: The water is hard to see through.
Tap each wavy scent clue.
Follow the trail to food.
story: Vision is limited in this murky coastal water.
Follow smell and vibration clues as the tiger shark searches for food.

mission.completion: Trail found! The tiger shark combined smell, hearing, and sight.

earlyReader: What marks a young tiger shark?
story: Which pattern is strongest on young tiger sharks?

questions.success: Yes! Young tiger sharks have dark stripes.
questions.retry: Look for the tiger-like pattern.

earlyReader: Which sense follows a scent?
story: Which sense helps track food through murky water?

questions.success: Right! Smell can follow a scent trail.
questions.retry: Choose the nose.

earlyReader: Where can tiger sharks live?
story: Which habitat may a tiger shark visit?

questions.success: That's it! Tiger sharks visit warm reefs.
questions.retry: Choose the warm place under the sea.

sharks.bookFact: Young tiger sharks have bold stripes that fade with age.

sharks.name: Epaulette Shark

earlyReader: This is an epaulette shark.
It has big black spots.
It can step across a reef with its fins.
story: The small spotted epaulette shark lives on shallow reef flats.
It uses sturdy paired fins to walk between tide pools.

earlyReader: Epaulette sharks live on shallow reefs.
Tides leave warm pools behind.
The shark stays near coral and sand.
story: Epaulette sharks live in warm, shallow reef flats around the Great Barrier Reef.
Falling tides can leave them inside isolated pools.

earlyReader: This shark searches under coral.
It eats worms, crabs, and shrimp.
Small crushing teeth help at dinner.
story: At dusk and night, epaulette sharks search for worms, shrimp, crabs, and small fish.
They poke beneath coral and seagrass to find prey.

earlyReader: This is a small shark.
It can be about as long as a child.
Its body is slim and flexible.
story: An epaulette shark grows to roughly one meter long.
Its slender, flexible body slips through tight reef spaces.

earlyReader: Smell helps it search.
Electric sensors spot hidden prey.
It explores mostly at night.
story: Smell and electroreception guide an epaulette shark toward hidden prey.
Those senses are useful during its nighttime search.

earlyReader: Strong front fins work like feet.
The shark steps over wet reef.
It can reach the next tide pool.
story: The epaulette braces on its pectoral and pelvic fins to walk.
It can cross exposed reef between tide pools at low tide.

traits.title: Reef Walker
traits.description: It uses its fins to push along shallow reef flats.

traits.title: Ocean Eye Spot
traits.description: A large dark spot near its fin may surprise a predator.

mission.title: Tide-Pool Trek

earlyReader: The tide is going out.
Tap each safe wet rock.
Help the shark step to the next pool.
story: Choose a damp path across the reef as the water falls.
Guide the epaulette shark from one tide pool to the next.

mission.completion: Step by step! The epaulette reached the next tide pool.

earlyReader: How does this shark move on a reef?
story: How can an epaulette cross exposed reef?

questions.success: Yes! It walks with strong paired fins.
questions.retry: Look for the stepping fins.

earlyReader: Where does it live?
story: Which home fits an epaulette shark?

questions.success: Right! It lives on a shallow reef.
questions.retry: Choose the coral place under warm water.

earlyReader: What grows in an egg case?
story: What hatches from an epaulette egg case?

questions.success: Exactly! A little shark pup hatches.
questions.retry: Look for the baby ocean animal.

sharks.bookFact: It can walk between tide pools using its paired fins.

sharks.name: Goblin Shark

earlyReader: This is a goblin shark.
It lives in the dark deep sea.
Its jaws can spring forward to strike.
story: The pinkish goblin shark cruises slowly through the dark deep sea.
When prey comes close, its remarkable jaws spring far forward.

earlyReader: Goblin sharks live very deep.
Sunlight cannot reach their home.
They stay near underwater slopes.
story: Goblin sharks live near deep seafloors and underwater slopes around the world.
Their habitat lies far below most sunlight.

earlyReader: Goblin sharks eat fish and squid.
Crustaceans can be food too.
The jaws snap prey from the dark.
story: Deep-sea fish, squid, and crustaceans become goblin shark prey.
Fast-projecting jaws help a slow swimmer capture nearby food.

earlyReader: This shark has a soft body.
Its long snout looks like a blade.
Its tail has a long top edge.
story: The goblin shark has a soft body, small fins, and a long flattened snout.
Its unusual shape suits a life in deep water.

earlyReader: The deep sea is dark.
Electric sensors cover the long snout.
They spot nearby living animals.
story: A goblin shark's long snout holds electroreceptors that sense living prey.
This electric sense works even in deep darkness.

earlyReader: The jaws rest under the head.
Then they spring far forward.
Thin teeth grab slippery prey.
story: A goblin shark launches both jaws forward in a rapid slingshot motion.
Long narrow teeth grasp prey before it can escape.

traits.title: Extendable Jaw
traits.description: Its jaws can shoot forward to grab food in deep water.

traits.title: Long Deep-Sea Snout
traits.description: Its long snout helps it sense food in the dark.

mission.title: Deep-Sea Jaw Strike

earlyReader: The deep water is dark.
Spot each electric clue.
Tap when the fish swims close.
story: Use the goblin shark's snout to sense prey in darkness.
Wait until each fish is close, then trigger the springing jaws.

mission.completion: Snap! The goblin shark's jaws sprang forward to catch prey.

earlyReader: Where does the goblin shark live?
story: Which habitat belongs to a goblin shark?

questions.success: Yes! It lives in the deep ocean.
questions.retry: Choose the darkest underwater place.

earlyReader: What can spring forward?
story: Which part shoots forward to capture prey?

questions.success: Right! Its jaws spring forward.
questions.retry: Look for the part with the teeth.

earlyReader: What helps it sense prey in darkness?
story: Which sense works when sunlight is gone?

questions.success: Exactly! Electric sensors find living prey.
questions.retry: Choose the electric sparkle.

sharks.bookFact: Its jaws launch forward like a slingshot in the deep sea.

sharks.name: Greenland Shark

earlyReader: This is a Greenland shark.
It stays in cold Arctic water.
It swims at a slow, steady speed.
story: The Greenland shark moves slowly through cold, deep Arctic water.
Its steady life may continue for several hundred years.

earlyReader: Greenland sharks like cold water.
They swim in the Arctic and North Atlantic.
Some stay very deep.
story: Greenland sharks inhabit the cold Arctic and North Atlantic oceans.
They often use deep water but may move shallower where water stays cold.

earlyReader: This shark eats fish and other animals.
It also finds animals already dead.
That makes it a scavenger too.
story: Greenland sharks eat fish and other marine animals while also scavenging carrion.
A strong sense of smell may guide them to distant food.

earlyReader: This shark can grow very large.
Its body is thick and heavy.
Small fins match its slow swim.
story: Greenland sharks have large, heavy bodies with relatively small fins.
Their shape and cold habitat fit a remarkably slow swimming style.

earlyReader: Dark water can hide food.
The shark follows a scent trail.
It stays steady and saves energy.
story: Smell helps Greenland sharks locate food in dark or cloudy water.
They follow clues without wasting energy on quick movement.

earlyReader: Cold water slows the shark's body.
It grows and moves slowly.
It may live for hundreds of years.
story: A very slow metabolism supports the Greenland shark's cold-water life.
Scientists estimate that this species can live for centuries.

traits.title: Slow and Steady
traits.description: Cold water slows its body, so it moves very gently.

traits.title: Arctic Home
traits.description: It lives in icy northern seas where few sharks can go.

mission.title: Arctic Scent Trail

earlyReader: Stay slow and steady.
Tap each snowy scent clue.
Save energy while you find food.
story: Follow a scent trail through cold Arctic darkness.
Move steadily from clue to clue without wasting the shark's energy.

mission.completion: Slow and steady! The Greenland shark followed the Arctic scent trail.

earlyReader: Which water does it like?
story: Which habitat suits a Greenland shark?

questions.success: Yes! Greenland sharks live in cold Arctic water.
questions.retry: Choose the icy ocean.

earlyReader: How does it usually swim?
story: Which movement saves energy in cold water?

questions.success: Right! It swims slowly and steadily.
questions.retry: Look for the movement that saves energy.

earlyReader: What is a scavenger?
story: Which food might a scavenger eat?

questions.success: Exactly! A scavenger eats animals it finds already dead.
questions.retry: Choose food the shark can find in the sea.

sharks.bookFact: This slow-growing Arctic shark may live for several hundred years.

vocabulary.explanation: Tiny living things that drift in water.
Straining tiny food from water.
Body parts that take oxygen from water.
An animal hunted for food.
Sensing tiny electric fields made by living things.
The ground at the bottom of the sea.
Soft feelers near an animal's mouth.
A pulling force made by moving water or air.
The place where an animal lives.
An animal that hunts other animals.
To travel from one region to another.
Colors and shapes that help an animal blend in.
Waiting hidden before a sudden hunt.
A group of fish swimming together.
To make an animal unable to move for a short time.
The fin that pushes a fish forward.
An animal that eats food it finds already dead.
A pool left among rocks when the tide falls.
One of the paired fins on a shark's sides.
A baby shark.
The dark ocean far below the surface.
The front part of an animal's head.
The part of the mouth that holds teeth.
The cold ocean region around the North Pole.
Living for a very long time.

earlyReader: Each whale shark has its own spot pattern.
Scientists take pictures of the spots.
The pictures help them recognize one shark again.
story: A whale shark's pale spots and stripes form a pattern that is unique to that individual.
Scientists use photographs of the pattern to recognize animals when they return to the same feeding place.

earlyReader: Trash can travel from land to the sea.
Plastic can hurt ocean animals.
We can use less plastic and put trash in the right bin.
story: Plastic and other marine debris can enter waterways and harm ocean animals.
Using reusable items and keeping litter out of drains helps protect feeding places for whale sharks and other sea life.

earlyReader: What helps scientists know one whale shark from another?
story: Which clue can help scientists recognize the same whale shark again?

questions.success: Yes! Its spots make a useful picture clue.
questions.retry: Look for the pale pattern on its back.

earlyReader: What helps keep the ocean safe?
story: Which choice helps prevent marine debris from reaching the ocean?

questions.success: Right! Properly tossing trash helps the ocean.
questions.retry: Choose the action that keeps trash out of water.

earlyReader: A hammerhead has a wide head.
Its eyes sit far apart at the ends.
The wide shape carries many electric sensors.
story: The hammer-shaped head is called a cephalofoil.
Its broad surface spreads the great hammerhead's eyes and electric sensors across a wide searching tool.

earlyReader: Sandy reefs are homes for many animals.
We can watch wild animals from a safe distance.
Clean water helps reef homes stay healthy.
story: Warm reefs and sandy coasts support many connected ocean animals.
Keeping waterways clean and giving wildlife space helps protect the places where hammerheads search for prey.

earlyReader: Where are a hammerhead's eyes?
story: Where do a great hammerhead's eyes sit on its wide head?

questions.success: Yes! Its eyes sit far apart on the wide head.
questions.retry: Think about the two far ends of the hammer shape.

earlyReader: How can we watch a wild shark?
story: Which choice respects ocean wildlife while people are visiting a reef?

questions.success: Right! Wild animals need space.
questions.retry: Choose the calm, respectful action.

earlyReader: Nurse sharks have small feelers by their mouths.
The feelers are called barbels.
They help the shark search near the seafloor.
story: A nurse shark has two short barbels near its mouth.
These touch-sensitive feelers help it investigate cracks and sand while searching for hidden food.

earlyReader: Coral reefs need clean water.
Trash does not belong on a reef.
A clean home helps nurse sharks and their neighbors.
story: Nurse sharks rest and feed around reefs and seagrass beds.
Keeping litter out of the water helps protect the many small animals that share those coastal homes.

earlyReader: What are the nurse shark's mouth feelers called?
story: What is the name for the small feelers near a nurse shark's mouth?

questions.success: Yes! Those feelers are barbels.
questions.retry: Choose the word for a shark's mouth feelers.

earlyReader: What belongs near a coral reef?
story: Which choice helps a reef stay a healthy home?

questions.success: Right! Reefs need clean water.
questions.retry: Choose the safe ocean home.

earlyReader: A great white has a pointed snout.
Its teeth are shaped for catching prey.
A strong tail pushes its big body through water.
story: Great white sharks have a streamlined body, a pointed snout, and a powerful tail.
Their triangular, serrated teeth help them hold and cut animal prey.

earlyReader: The open ocean is a busy animal home.
Wildlife needs clean water and space.
We can keep trash out of rivers and beaches.
story: Great whites travel through coastal and open-ocean habitats.
Keeping debris out of waterways and respecting wildlife from a distance supports healthier ocean places.

earlyReader: What helps push a great white forward?
story: Which body part provides a great white shark's strong swimming push?

questions.success: Yes! Its tail gives a powerful push.
questions.retry: Look behind the shark for the swimming engine.

earlyReader: Where should beach trash go?
story: Which action helps protect an ocean animal's home?

questions.success: Right! A bin keeps trash out of the sea.
questions.retry: Choose the place made for trash.

earlyReader: A wobbegong has little skin flaps by its mouth.
The flaps break up its outline.
They help the shark look like part of the seafloor.
story: Spotted wobbegongs have branching skin lobes around the mouth and a patterned body.
Together, these features help blur the shark's outline against rocks, sand, and reef.

earlyReader: The seafloor is a busy home.
Animals resting there need clean water.
We can leave wild places clean and quiet.
story: A wobbegong depends on a healthy seafloor for camouflage and hunting.
Keeping litter out of the ocean and observing wildlife without disturbance helps protect that habitat.

earlyReader: What helps break up a wobbegong's outline?
story: Which body feature helps a wobbegong look like the seafloor?

questions.success: Yes! Skin flaps help its disguise.
questions.retry: Look for the leafy-looking parts near its mouth.

earlyReader: How should we treat a wild seafloor home?
story: Which choice helps an animal that rests on the seafloor?

questions.success: Right! Clean, quiet homes help wildlife.
questions.retry: Choose the gentle ocean action.

earlyReader: A thresher's top tail lobe is extra long.
It swings the tail like a whip.
The splash can stun small fish.
story: The thresher shark's upper tail lobe can be nearly as long as its body.
It uses that remarkable tail to strike close schools of fish and make prey easier to catch.

earlyReader: Open water is an animal home too.
Trash can drift a long way there.
Using less plastic helps keep water safer.
story: Ocean currents can carry marine debris far from where it first enters the water.
Reducing plastic waste helps protect open-ocean animals such as thresher sharks and their prey.

earlyReader: Which tail part is extra long?
story: What makes a thresher shark's tail especially useful for hunting?

questions.success: Yes! The top tail lobe is extra long.
questions.retry: Think about the long upper part of its tail.

earlyReader: Why use less plastic?
story: Which choice helps keep drifting trash out of open ocean water?

questions.success: Right! Reusing can reduce plastic waste.
questions.retry: Choose the action that makes less trash.

earlyReader: Young tiger sharks have dark stripes.
The stripes can fade as they grow.
Their broad heads help make a strong shape.
story: Young tiger sharks are marked with dark bars and spots that give the species its name.
The pattern often becomes less bold as the shark grows larger.

earlyReader: Coasts connect land and sea.
Trash on a street can reach the water.
Keeping drains clear helps ocean animals.
story: Storm drains and streams can carry litter from land toward coastal water.
Putting waste in the right bin helps protect the murky bays and reefs that tiger sharks visit.

earlyReader: What can happen to tiger shark stripes as it grows?
story: How can a tiger shark's dark pattern change as the animal grows?

questions.success: Yes! The stripes can fade with age.
questions.retry: Choose the change that happens to a color pattern.

earlyReader: What can carry litter toward the ocean?
story: Which place can send street litter toward coastal water?

questions.success: Right! Drains can lead toward waterways.
questions.retry: Look for the place rainwater flows into.

earlyReader: An epaulette has sturdy paired fins.
It braces on the fins to move.
That helps it step across wet reef.
story: The epaulette shark uses its pectoral and pelvic fins in a walking motion.
This unusual movement helps it travel between shallow tide pools when water is low.

earlyReader: Tide pools are small ocean homes.
Tiny animals live between the rocks.
We can look gently and leave the pool clean.
story: Tide pools shelter many small animals when the tide falls.
Watching carefully, leaving animals where they are, and keeping litter away helps protect these shallow reef homes.

earlyReader: Which fins help an epaulette walk?
story: Which body parts does an epaulette shark brace on to walk across a reef?

questions.success: Yes! Strong paired fins help it step.
questions.retry: Choose the fins on the shark's sides.

earlyReader: How should we explore a tide pool?
story: Which choice respects the animals living in a tide pool?

questions.success: Right! Gentle looking protects tide-pool animals.
questions.retry: Choose the kind action.

earlyReader: A goblin shark has a long flat snout.
Tiny electric sensors cover the snout.
Its jaws can shoot forward under the head.
story: The goblin shark's long flattened snout contains many electroreceptors.
When prey is close, its jaws can project forward in a dramatic slingshot motion.

earlyReader: The deep ocean is far away but still connected.
Trash can sink or drift through water.
Using less plastic helps every ocean zone.
story: Deep-sea habitats are connected to the rest of the ocean by moving water and sinking material.
Preventing debris before it reaches the sea is one way people can help protect animals far below the surface.

earlyReader: What covers a goblin shark's long snout?
story: Which special sensors cover the goblin shark's long snout?

questions.success: Yes! Electric sensors cover its snout.
questions.retry: Choose the sparkly sensing clue.

earlyReader: Does the deep ocean connect to other water?
story: Which statement is true about the deep ocean and the rest of the sea?

questions.success: Right! Ocean water connects many places.
questions.retry: Think about water moving through one big ocean.

earlyReader: A Greenland shark has a thick body.
Small fins match its slow swim.
Cold water slows its body processes.
story: The Greenland shark has a heavy body and relatively small fins for its size.
Its cold-water life is linked to a very slow metabolism, movement, and growth rate.

earlyReader: Cold ocean water is an animal home.
Trash can travel to faraway coasts.
Using reusable things helps make less waste.
story: Ocean currents can move debris far from where people first drop it.
Choosing reusable items and disposing of waste properly helps protect cold-water habitats as well as warm ones.

earlyReader: What slows a Greenland shark's body processes?
story: What is closely linked to the Greenland shark's slow metabolism?

questions.success: Yes! Cold water is part of its slow life.
questions.retry: Choose the icy habitat clue.

earlyReader: What can make less ocean trash?
story: Which choice can reduce the amount of disposable waste people make?

questions.success: Right! Reusing can make less waste.
questions.retry: Choose the arrow that means use again.
